title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Rising LCFS Trade Volumes Amid Price Stability and Strategic Positioning
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(2nd December â€“ 8th December)
  • A total of 531,708 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 83.6% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades increased by 124.7% WoW as 1,540,517 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased marginally this week to $76.62/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 0.01%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers declined by 4.51% in price, reaching $66.91/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 0.39% WoW; On 11th Dec, the spot price stood at $75.63/MT. The benchmark price stood at $76.38/MT on 11th
  • D3 RIN prices marginally increase by 0.4% WoW, and D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each rose by 3.33% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California LCFS market witnessed a significant surge in trade volumes this week in both Type 1 and total non-zero trades. This heightened activity reflects seasonal end-of-year liquidity trends as entities finalize their credit portfolios ahead of compliance deadlines. Additionally, market participants appear to be positioning themselves strategically for upcoming regulatory changes, including stricter CI benchmarks and expanded feedstock pathways. Despite the surge in trading, credit prices declined, with Type 1 credit prices dipping slightly and all non-zero trades seeing a larger decrease. Spot credit prices showed relative stability. These price trends suggest that supply currently exceeds demand, exerting downward pressure on prices. The increased trade volumes amid declining prices highlight a short-term balancing act in the market. Participants may be leveraging the current price dip to secure credits cost-effectively before anticipated tightening in credit availability due to regulatory updates. As stricter CI benchmarks and accelerated ZEV adoption targets are implemented, credit supply is expected to tighten, potentially driving prices upward in the near future. This dynamic could further encourage strategic trading and feedstock diversification as stakeholders adjust to the evolving regulatory framework.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_590529" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.01% WoW fall, decreasing from $76.63 to $76.62 this week, while all non-zero credit prices declined by 4.51% WoW, dropping from $70.07 to $66.91. Type 1 trades saw 531,708 credits exchanged, reflecting a 83.60% WoW increase. Similarly, the total transaction volume for all non-zero trades surged 1.25x times WoW to reach 1,540,517 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_590530" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased marginally by 0.39% WoW, reaching $77.18/MT. On 11th December, the average spot price was $75.63/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $76.38/MT as of 11th December.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_590531" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 80 trades were reported in the market, with fifteen high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $84.00 for two Type 2 transactions with a volume of 5,000 and 18,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $41.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for two transactions at a volume of 15,000 and 34,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 452 credits of Type 1 category, were undertaken for price of $74.00 whereas the largest volume of transfer was reported at 295,000 MT of Type 1 category at a price of $76.69.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_590532" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Trading volumes in November 2024 declined significantly, despite rising prices. November recorded a volume of just 2.01 MMT, a sharp drop from Octoberâ€™s record of 6.4 MMT. Average Type 1 prices for November increased to $73.07/MT, compared to Octoberâ€™s $65.93/MT. Early December has seen a continued upward trend in Type 1 prices.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_590533" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 02nd Â Dec â€“ 08th Dec, total VPT recorded a Â 21.31% decline (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increase marginally by 0.41% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 3.33% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Â 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Vision RNG Announces 25 Year Gas Agreement with Greenwood County South Carolina Landfill : Vision RNG (VRNG) has announced a long-term gas rights agreement to purchase raw landfill gas (LFG) from the Greenwood County South Carolina landfill located in Greenwood South Carolina. The agreement could be as long as 25 years. A natural byproduct of decomposing landfill waste, LFG is typically collected and flared to burn off the methane it contains as required by regulators. However, collecting and converting the methane present in LFG into a cleaner, renewable fuel is intended to serve as a solution to reduce the need for combustion of new fossil fuels.
  • U.S. Department of the Treasury Releases Final Rules on Investment Tax Credit to Produce Clean Power, Strengthen Clean Energy Economy : The U.S. Department of the Treasury and the IRS released final rules for the Section 48 Energy Credit â€“ also known as the Investment Tax Credit (ITC) â€“ that will give clean energy project developers clarity and certainty to undertake major investments to produce more clean power and further strengthen Americaâ€™s clean energy economy.
